planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; Caloro is a yellow wax hot pepper in the Hungarian Wax style developed in California, with conical yellow fruits that ripen to red and carry moderate heat of 5,000–10,000 Scoville units — excellent for pickling, roasting, and fresh use in California-Mexican cuisine.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost when nighttime temperatures are consistently above 55°F into fertile, well-drained soil in full sun. Caloro is a yellow wax-type hot pepper developed in California in the mid-twentieth century and widely grown in commercial agriculture as the standard pickled yellow hot pepper of California; the conical, medium-sized fruits (3–4 inches long) emerge green, ripen to bright yellow (the commercial harvest stage for pickling), and eventually turn orange-red at full maturity. Heat level is moderate at 5,000–10,000 SHU — noticeably hot but not extreme — with a waxy, crisp texture ideal for pickled pepper rings, banana pepper-style pickles, roasting, and fresh eating. Highly productive and disease-resistant; particularly well-suited to warm California, Gulf Coast, and Southwestern climates.
sources: University of California Cooperative Extension Vegetable Research

planting.note: Start on a heat mat 8–10 weeks before last frost; Aji Panca is one of Peru's most important culinary peppers, a large, deep red to dark chocolate-red chile with mild heat (1,500–2,500 SHU) and an exceptional smoky, fruity, berry-like flavor used as the base of many traditional Peruvian sauces, marinades, and stews including aji de gallina.

directions: Start seeds on a heat mat at 80°F 8–10 weeks before last frost; transplant after frost when soil is warm. Aji Panca is one of the most important and distinctive chiles in Peruvian cuisine, a large (3–4 inch), elongated, deep brownish-red pepper belonging to Capsicum chinense but with very mild heat (1,500–2,500 SHU) and an extraordinary flavor profile: smoky, deeply fruity, berry-like, and slightly sweet with almost no raw pungency — it is more like a richly flavored dried fruit than a typical hot pepper. In Peru it is primarily used dried and pureed as a chile paste (pasta de aji panca) that forms the flavor base of marinades, braises, anticuchos (grilled beef hearts), and the classic preparation aji de gallina. Rarely found outside Latin American grocery stores in the US; growing your own from seed is the only way to access this unique pepper fresh in most American gardens.
sources: Dave DeWitt's The Chile Pepper Encyclopedia

planting.note: Start on a heat mat 6–8 weeks before last frost; Rocotillo is a traditional Caribbean seasoning pepper related to the habanero but with minimal heat (1,500–2,500 SHU), prized entirely for its intensely tropical, fruity habanero aroma and flavor with none of the fire — a favorite seasoning pepper in Cuban, Puerto Rican, and Dominican cooking.

directions: Germinate on a heat mat at 80°F; transplant outdoors after frost when soil is warm and nights are above 60°F. Rocotillo is a Capsicum chinense seasoning pepper of Caribbean origin that looks remarkably like a miniature, squashed habanero with its distinctive ribbed, round, flattened form — but carries minimal heat (1,500–2,500 SHU) while retaining the intensely fruity, tropical, citrusy habanero aroma that makes this species unique; it is used in Cuban, Puerto Rican, Dominican, and Venezuelan cooking wherever the habanero flavor without the fire is desired. The small, round, ribbed fruits ripen from green through yellow to orange or red and are used fresh in sofrito, salsas, sauces, and seasoning blends. Related to and sometimes confused with the aji dulce (sweet habanero) and cachucha peppers of the Caribbean. Highly productive in hot weather; perennial in zone 9b–12.
sources: Dave DeWitt's The Chile Pepper Encyclopedia
University of the West Indies Agricultural Research

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; Cherry Bomb is an All-America Selections winner with round, cherry-like fruits ripening to red, moderate heat of 2,500–5,000 SHU, and thick walls ideal for fresh eating, pickling, and stuffing — one of the most versatile and productive hot-pepper garden standards.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant outdoors after frost in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun. Cherry Bomb is an All-America Selections winning pepper producing compact, globe-shaped, 1.5–2 inch fruits that resemble large cherries; at 2,500–5,000 SHU (mild-to-medium heat) it is significantly hotter than banana peppers but much milder than jalapeños, with thick, juicy, crunchy walls and excellent sweet pepper flavor with a moderate bite. Fruits ripen from green to brilliant red and are prized for stuffing whole (the round shape makes perfect stuffed cherry peppers), pickling in oil and vinegar in the Italian style, and fresh slicing. Compact, bush-type plants are highly productive and perform well in containers. Widely available in the Italian-American food tradition as pickled cherry peppers in antipasto.

planting.note: Start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; Espelette is the Basque Country's PDO (Protected Designation of Origin) chile pepper, grown in the Espelette region of southwest France since Basque explorers brought capsicums from the Americas in the 16th century and now the defining spice of Basque cuisine.

directions: Start seeds indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost; transplant into fertile, well-drained soil in full sun after all frost danger has passed. Piment d'Espelette is the unique chile pepper of the Espelette commune and neighboring villages in the Pays Basque region of southwest France and northern Spain, where it holds PDO (Appellation d'Origine Protégée) status — meaning the authentic product can only come from that specific area. The elongated, 3–5 inch, initially green peppers ripen to brilliant red and are traditionally strung in long chains and dried in the mountain air; the resulting dried, ground powder has a distinctive mild-to-medium heat (1,500–4,000 SHU), sweet pepper aroma, and earthy-fruity flavor that is unlike any other pepper spice. Used to season virtually all savory foods in Basque cooking in place of black pepper — on eggs, fish, piperade, axoa (veal stew), chicken basquaise, and fromage de brebis (Basque sheep cheese). Grow in any warm garden; the fresh peppers have excellent flavor even without the Basque drying climate.
sources: Institut National de l'Origine et de la Qualité (INAO) France
Dave DeWitt's The Chile Pepper Encyclopedia
